mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that provides declarative APIs and tooling for managing the lifecycle of Kubernetes clusters. It simplifies provisioning, upgrading, and operating multiple clusters across various infrastructure environments. Key trade-offs include the need for a deeper understanding of Kubernetes concepts and potential complexity in managing multiple providers.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This repository contains the necessary charts for installation.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are required for Cluster API to function correctly as they define the custom resources used by the operator.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.5/cluster-api.crds.yaml

```

> This command installs the required CRDs.

steps.title: Install the Cluster API components
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API controller components into the specified namespace.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.5

```

> Ensure you are installing in the kube-system namespace.

steps.title: Create a Cluster API custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a custom resource that defines the desired state of your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

```

> Replace 'MyInfrastructureProviderCluster' with your specific infrastructure provider.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Cluster API components to ensure they are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Look for the Cluster API controller pods and ensure they are in 'Running' state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API components
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Cluster API components and CRDs. Be aware that this may lead to data loss of any managed clusters.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.5/cluster-api.crds.yaml

```

> This command removes the Cluster API Helm release.
> This command deletes the CRDs associated with Cluster API.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your cluster configurations. This step will upgrade the Cluster API components to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get clusters -o yaml > clusters-backup.yaml

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.5

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

```

> Backup your current cluster configurations.
> This command upgrades the Cluster API to the specified version.
> Verify that all pods are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster CRD is not installed or not available.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.5/cluster-api.crds.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'context deadline exceeded'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates that the Cluster API controller is not responding or is down.

**Fix:**
Check the logs of the Cluster API controller pods using: kubectl logs -n kube-system <pod-name>

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to create cluster: infrastructure provider not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the specified infrastructure provider is not installed or configured correctly.

**Fix:**
Ensure the correct infrastructure provider is installed and referenced in your Cluster custom resource.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates a problem with the Kubernetes API server certificate.

**Fix:**
Check your kubeconfig and ensure that the cluster certificates are valid.

versionNotes.changes: Improved support for new infrastructure providers, added new flags for better customization.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the old API versions for Cluster and Machine resources.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.11.x, ensure to update your custom resources to the new API versions.

resolution.summary: You should see the Cluster API controller pods running in the kube-system namespace with a status of 'Running'. Use 'kubectl get clusters' to verify that your custom resources are created successfully.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  infrastructureRef:
    apiVersion: infrastructure.cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
    kind: MyInfrastructureProviderCluster
    name: my-infrastructure
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.26 or higher and the necessary tools installed.
